verses.text: In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah saw the Lord high and lifted up, His train filling the temple, while seraphim cried, 'Holy, holy, holy.' He beheld God's glory. The vision of the thrice-holy God overwhelms every earthly throne.

In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah saw the Lord high and lifted up, His train filling the temple, while seraphim cried, 'Holy, holy, holy.' He beheld God's glory. The vision of the thrice-holy God overwhelms every earthly throne.

In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah saw the Lord high and lifted up, His train filling the temple, while seraphim cried, 'Holy, holy, holy.' He beheld God's glory. The vision of the thrice-holy God overwhelms every earthly throne.

In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah saw the Lord high and lifted up, His train filling the temple, while seraphim cried, 'Holy, holy, holy.' He beheld God's glory. The vision of the thrice-holy God overwhelms every earthly throne.
'Woe is me! for I am undone... a man of unclean lips,' Isaiah cries, for his eyes have seen the King. Nearness to holiness exposes sin. To truly see God is to feel one's own uncleanness.

A seraph touches his lips with a live coal from the altar: 'thine iniquity is taken away, and thy sin purged.' Cleansing comes from the altar. God Himself removes the guilt of the soul that confesses it.

A seraph touches his lips with a live coal from the altar: 'thine iniquity is taken away, and thy sin purged.' Cleansing comes from the altar. God Himself removes the guilt of the soul that confesses it.
The Lord asks, 'Whom shall I send?' and Isaiah answers, 'Here am I; send me.' Cleansed, he is ready to serve. The pardoned soul gladly offers itself for God's errands.

He is sent to a people who will hear yet not understand, their hearts made fat by their own rejection. Persistent unbelief hardens. The word that should save becomes, to those who refuse it, a sentence of blindness.

He is sent to a people who will hear yet not understand, their hearts made fat by their own rejection. Persistent unbelief hardens. The word that should save becomes, to those who refuse it, a sentence of blindness.

Asked how long, the Lord answers, until the land is wasted; yet a tenth, 'a holy seed,' shall remain like a stump that lives. Judgment is not the end. God preserves a holy remnant from which life springs again.
